questions.question: How does a local (stdio) MCP server communicate with its client?
options: Over a Unix socket with protobuf frames
It is spawned as a child process; newline-delimited JSON-RPC flows over stdin/stdout
Via a local HTTP server on port 8080
Through shared memory
questions.explanation: The client spawns the server as a child process and exchanges one JSON object per line over stdin/stdout. The 2025-11-25 spec uses explicit newline separation — no length-prefixed framing, and no SSE (that legacy remote mode is being removed in mid-2026).

questions.question: Why must a stdio MCP server never print debug output to stdout?
options: Stdout is reserved for JSON-RPC envelopes — anything else corrupts the protocol stream; logs go to stderr
Stdout is too slow for logging
The spec requires logs be written to a file
Clients automatically kill servers that log
questions.explanation: Stdout IS the wire. A stray print statement injects non-JSON bytes into the message stream and breaks the client's parser. Debug logs belong on stderr; also flush stdout after every write.

questions.question: MCP distinguishes protocol-level errors from tool-level errors. How is each returned?
options: Both as HTTP status codes
Protocol errors (unknown method, bad params) as JSON-RPC error objects; tool failures as a normal result with isError: true so the model can see and react to the failure
Both crash the server, which the client restarts
Tool errors as JSON-RPC errors; protocol errors as plain text
questions.explanation: A valid call whose tool failed returns {content: [...], isError: true} — putting the failure into the model's context so it can adjust. JSON-RPC error envelopes are reserved for protocol violations like unknown methods.

questions.question: What does tools/call return on success?
options: A plain string
{content: [typed blocks like text, resource, image], isError: false}
An HTTP 200 with no body
The raw return value of the Python function
questions.explanation: Results are arrays of typed content blocks — {type: "text"}, {type: "resource", resource: {uri, text}}, {type: "image", data, mimeType} — letting one call return rich, mixed content.

questions.question: Why is a notes server the teaching example for MCP servers?
options: Notes are the only data MCP supports
It exercises all three server primitives: tools for mutations, resources for data (notes://{id}), prompts for templates
It requires no error handling
Anthropic mandates it as the certification project
questions.explanation: notes_create is a mutating tool, notes://14 is a readable resource, and review_note is a prompt template surfaced as a slash-command — one small domain covering the full server surface, generalizable to any real integration.
